lines.speaker: RODERIGO

lines.text: ’Sblood, but you will not hear me:

lines.speaker: RODERIGO

lines.speaker: IAGO

lines.speaker: IAGO

lines.speaker: IAGO

lines.speaker: IAGO

lines.speaker: IAGO

lines.speaker: IAGO

lines.speaker: IAGO

lines.speaker: IAGO

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: At Rhodes, at Cyprus and on other grounds

lines.speaker: IAGO

lines.speaker: IAGO

lines.speaker: IAGO

lines.speaker: IAGO

lines.speaker: RODERIGO
lines.text: By heaven, I rather would have been his hangman.

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: Why, there’s no remedy; ’tis the curse of service,

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: Preferment goes by letter and affection,

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: And not by old gradation, where each second

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: Stood heir to the first. Now, sir, be judge yourself,

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: Whether I in any just term am affined

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: To love the Moor.

lines.speaker: RODERIGO
lines.text: I would not follow him then.

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: O, sir, content you;

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: I follow him to serve my turn upon him:

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: We cannot all be masters, nor all masters

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: Cannot be truly follow’d. You shall mark

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: Many a duteous and knee-crooking knave,

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: That, doting on his own obsequious bondage,

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: Wears out his time, much like his master’s ass,

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: For nought but provender, and when he’s old, cashier’d:

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: Whip me such honest knaves. Others there are

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: Who, trimm’d in forms and visages of duty,

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: Keep yet their hearts attending on themselves,

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: And, throwing but shows of service on their lords,

lines.speaker: IAGO

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: their coats

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: Do themselves homage: these fellows have some soul;

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: And such a one do I profess myself. For, sir,

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: It is as sure as you are Roderigo,

lines.speaker: IAGO

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: In following him, I follow but myself;

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: Heaven is my judge, not I for love and duty,

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: But seeming so, for my peculiar end:

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: For when my outward action doth demonstrate

lines.speaker: IAGO

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: For daws to peck at: I am not what I am.

lines.speaker: RODERIGO
lines.text: If he can carry’t thus!

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: Call up her father,

lines.speaker: IAGO

lines.speaker: IAGO

lines.speaker: IAGO

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: Do, with like timorous accent and dire yell

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: As when, by night and negligence, the fire

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: Is spied in populous cities.

lines.speaker: RODERIGO
lines.text: What, ho, Brabantio! Signior Brabantio, ho!

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: Awake! what, ho, Brabantio! thieves! thieves! thieves!

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: Look to your house, your daughter and your bags!

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: Thieves! thieves!

lines.speaker: BRABANTIO
lines.text: What is the reason of this terrible summons?

lines.speaker: BRABANTIO
lines.text: What is the matter there?

lines.speaker: RODERIGO
lines.text: Signior, is all your family within?

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: Are your doors lock’d?

lines.speaker: BRABANTIO
lines.text: Why, wherefore ask you this?

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: ’Zounds, sir, you’re robb’d; for shame, put on your gown;

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: Your heart is burst, you have lost half your soul;

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: Even now, now, very now, an old black ram

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: Is topping your white ewe. Arise, arise;

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: Awake the snorting citizens with the bell,

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: Or else the devil will make a grandsire of you:

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: Arise, I say.

lines.speaker: BRABANTIO
lines.text: What, have you lost your wits?

lines.speaker: RODERIGO
lines.text: Most reverend signior, do you know my voice?

lines.speaker: BRABANTIO
lines.text: Not I what are you?

lines.speaker: RODERIGO
lines.text: My name is Roderigo.

lines.speaker: BRABANTIO
lines.text: The worser welcome:

lines.speaker: BRABANTIO
lines.text: I have charged thee not to haunt about my doors:

lines.speaker: BRABANTIO
lines.text: In honest plainness thou hast heard me say

lines.speaker: BRABANTIO
lines.text: My daughter is not for thee; and now, in madness,

lines.speaker: BRABANTIO
lines.text: Being full of supper and distempering draughts,

lines.speaker: BRABANTIO
lines.text: Upon malicious bravery, dost thou come

lines.speaker: BRABANTIO
lines.text: To start my quiet.

lines.speaker: RODERIGO
lines.text: Sir, sir, sir—

lines.speaker: BRABANTIO
lines.text: But thou must needs be sure

lines.speaker: BRABANTIO
lines.text: My spirit and my place have in them power

lines.speaker: BRABANTIO
lines.text: To make this bitter to thee.

lines.speaker: RODERIGO
lines.text: Patience, good sir.

lines.speaker: BRABANTIO
lines.text: What tell’st thou me of robbing? this is Venice;

lines.speaker: BRABANTIO
lines.text: My house is not a grange.

lines.speaker: RODERIGO
lines.text: Most grave Brabantio,

lines.speaker: RODERIGO
lines.text: In simple and pure soul I come to you.

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: ’Zounds, sir, you are one of those that will not serve God,

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: if the devil bid you. Because we come to do you service

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: and you think we are ruffians, you’ll have your daughter covered with

lines.speaker: IAGO

lines.speaker: IAGO

lines.speaker: BRABANTIO
lines.text: What profane wretch art thou?

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: I am one, sir, that comes to tell you your daughter and

lines.speaker: IAGO

lines.speaker: BRABANTIO
lines.text: Thou art a villain.

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: You are—a senator.

lines.speaker: BRABANTIO
lines.text: This thou shalt answer; I know thee, Roderigo.

lines.speaker: RODERIGO
lines.text: Sir, I will answer any thing. But, I beseech you,

lines.speaker: RODERIGO
lines.text: If’t be your pleasure and most wise consent,

lines.speaker: RODERIGO
lines.text: As partly I find it is, that your fair daughter,

lines.speaker: RODERIGO
lines.text: At this odd-even and dull watch o’ the night,

lines.speaker: RODERIGO

lines.speaker: RODERIGO

lines.speaker: RODERIGO

lines.speaker: RODERIGO

lines.speaker: RODERIGO

lines.speaker: RODERIGO
lines.text: But if you know not this, my manners tell me

lines.speaker: RODERIGO

lines.speaker: RODERIGO
lines.text: That, from the sense of all civility,

lines.speaker: RODERIGO
lines.text: I thus would play and trifle with your reverence:

lines.speaker: RODERIGO

lines.speaker: RODERIGO
lines.text: I say again, hath made a gross revolt;

lines.speaker: RODERIGO
lines.text: Tying her duty, beauty, wit and fortunes

lines.speaker: RODERIGO
lines.text: In an extravagant and wheeling stranger

lines.speaker: RODERIGO
lines.text: Of here and every where. Straight satisfy yourself:

lines.speaker: RODERIGO
lines.text: If she be in her chamber or your house,

lines.speaker: RODERIGO
lines.text: Let loose on me the justice of the state

lines.speaker: RODERIGO
lines.text: For thus deluding you.

lines.speaker: BRABANTIO
lines.text: Strike on the tinder, ho!

lines.speaker: BRABANTIO
lines.text: Give me a taper! call up all my people!

lines.speaker: BRABANTIO
lines.text: This accident is not unlike my dream:

lines.speaker: BRABANTIO
lines.text: Belief of it oppresses me already.

lines.speaker: BRABANTIO
lines.text: Light, I say! light!

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: Farewell; for I must leave you:

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: It seems not meet, nor wholesome to my place,

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: To be produced—as, if I stay, I shall—

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: Against the Moor: for, I do know, the state,

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: However this may gall him with some cheque,

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: Cannot with safety cast him, for he’s embark’d

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: With such loud reason to the Cyprus wars,

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: Which even now stand in act, that, for their souls,

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: Another of his fathom they have none,

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: To lead their business: in which regard,

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: Though I do hate him as I do hell-pains.

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: Yet, for necessity of present life,

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: I must show out a flag and sign of love,

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: Which is indeed but sign. That you shall surely find him,

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: Lead to the Sagittary the raised search;

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: And there will I be with him. So, farewell.

lines.speaker: BRABANTIO
lines.text: It is too true an evil: gone she is;

lines.speaker: BRABANTIO
lines.text: And what’s to come of my despised time

lines.speaker: BRABANTIO
lines.text: Is nought but bitterness. Now, Roderigo,

lines.speaker: BRABANTIO
lines.text: Where didst thou see her? O unhappy girl!

lines.speaker: BRABANTIO
lines.text: With the Moor, say’st thou? Who would be a father!

lines.speaker: BRABANTIO
lines.text: How didst thou know ’twas she? O she deceives me

lines.speaker: BRABANTIO
lines.text: Past thought! What said she to you? Get more tapers:

lines.speaker: BRABANTIO
lines.text: Raise all my kindred. Are they married, think you?

lines.speaker: RODERIGO
lines.text: Truly, I think they are.

lines.speaker: BRABANTIO
lines.text: O heaven! How got she out? O treason of the blood!

lines.speaker: BRABANTIO
lines.text: Fathers, from hence trust not your daughters’ minds

lines.speaker: BRABANTIO
lines.text: By what you see them act. Is there not charms

lines.speaker: BRABANTIO
lines.text: By which the property of youth and maidhood

lines.speaker: BRABANTIO
lines.text: May be abused? Have you not read, Roderigo,

lines.speaker: BRABANTIO
lines.text: Of some such thing?

lines.speaker: RODERIGO
lines.text: Yes, sir, I have indeed.

lines.speaker: BRABANTIO
lines.text: Call up my brother. O, would you had had her!

lines.speaker: BRABANTIO
lines.text: Some one way, some another. Do you know

lines.speaker: BRABANTIO
lines.text: Where we may apprehend her and the Moor?

lines.speaker: RODERIGO
lines.text: I think I can discover him, if you please,

lines.speaker: RODERIGO
lines.text: To get good guard and go along with me.

lines.speaker: BRABANTIO
lines.text: Pray you, lead on. At every house I’ll call;

lines.speaker: BRABANTIO
lines.text: I may command at most. Get weapons, ho!

lines.speaker: BRABANTIO
lines.text: And raise some special officers of night.

lines.speaker: BRABANTIO
lines.text: On, good Roderigo: I’ll deserve your pains.

stage_directions.text: Brabantio appears above, at a window.
Enter, below, Brabantio, and Servants with torches.

glosses.definition: an exclamation of impatience or dismissal; roughly 'oh please' or 'nonsense'
by God's blood — a strong oath, roughly 'damn it'
loathe; despise utterly
prominent citizens; members of Venice's ruling class
a personal petition or request for advancement
inflated, evasive talk; padding meant to delay a clear answer
military terminology; technical terms of the soldier's trade
scornful: a theorist who knows war only from books and ledgers, not the field
how to array troops in formation for combat
Iago's mocking coinage, substituting 'Moor' for 'Lordship' to belittle Othello
ensign; standard-bearer — the junior commissioned officer in a company
written recommendation and personal favoritism
the traditional, orderly sequence of promotion by seniority

glosses.phrase: throwing but shows of service on their lords
glosses.definition: giving only the outward display of service to their masters

glosses.phrase: native act and figure of my heart
glosses.definition: the real actions and outward form of my true feelings
jackdaws; small crow-like birds — here, fools or scavengers
tone of alarm; a voice raised in fear
snoring
by God's wounds — a vehement oath, roughly 'damn' or 'my God'
bravado; ostentatious defiance
a North African stallion — Iago's insult comparing Othello to a foreign animal and threatening Brabantio with mixed-race descendants
your grandchildren will be horses — continuation of the Barbary-horse slur
fast horses for kinsmen; the insult extends — your family will be a stable

glosses.phrase: making the beast with two backs
glosses.definition: proverbial Elizabethan figure for sexual intercourse — two bodies joined, appearing as a single beast with two backs
an isolated country house or outlying farm
blasphemous; outrageous in speech
a Venetian boatman — a hired man of no standing, underscoring the secret nature of the elopement
an inn in Venice, so named for a centaur-archer sign — where Othello lodges
a search party roused from bed
brought forth as evidence
depth of capacity; scope of ability

annotations.title: Beginning in the middle
annotations.body:
Shakespeare opens in medias res. Iago is already scheming, Roderigo is already the dupe, and the elopement has already happened offstage. The audience is handed consequences without premises and must infer cause from effect — a structural trick that mirrors Iago's own methods of suggestion and withheld information. The technique cultivates suspicion in the audience at the same moment Iago cultivates it on stage. The play will not stop asking us to mistrust what we think we have seen.

Compare the slow exposition of *Hamlet* I.i (the ghost, the watch, the political frame) with this scene's sprint into grievance and the whispered summons to Brabantio. Othello will not appear for nearly two hundred lines; by the time he arrives we have been steeped in his enemy's version of him.

annotations.title: Iago's grievance and the Venetian officer corps
annotations.body:
Iago calls Cassio an 'arithmetician' — a theorist who knows war from books and ledgers. The slur is professional as well as personal: Venice had a sophisticated military bureaucracy and promoted by commission rather than seniority, exactly as Iago complains. This was a genuine tension in early-modern European armies, and Shakespeare's audience would have recognized the type of grievance — an ensign passed over for a younger officer whose patron had weight in the signiory.

The point matters because it grounds Iago's malice in something recognizable, not diabolical. His opening move toward the audience is to sound reasonable. The play will spend much of its length revising what 'reasonable' means.
sources: Michael Mallett & John Hale, *The Military Organization of a Renaissance State* (1984)

annotations.title: The rhetoric of racial terror
annotations.body:
Under cover of darkness, Iago shouts a catalogue of animal and sexual images at Brabantio: the black ram, the white ewe, the Barbary horse, coursers for cousins, the beast with two backs. This is not casual color-blindness disrupted by a single slur but a sustained rhetorical program. Every image conflates blackness with bestiality, miscegenation with debasement, the body of Othello with foreign herds and breeding stock.

Iago does not expect Brabantio to believe these precise metaphors; he expects the metaphors to do their work below belief. Where *Titus Andronicus* and *The Merchant of Venice* had racialized villains, *Othello* begins by racializing its protagonist through the voice of his enemy — teaching the audience to see him first through Iago's lens. The play will then labor, not always successfully, to disconfirm what this opening has instilled.
sources: Kim F. Hall, *Things of Darkness: Economies of Race and Gender in Early Modern England* (1995)
Virginia Mason Vaughan, *Performing Blackness on English Stages, 1500–1800* (2005)

annotations.title: Iago's first exit and the play's architecture
annotations.body: At line 160 Iago slips offstage before Brabantio sees him, reappearing in the next scene beside Othello as though he had been there all along. This will become the play's signature manoeuvre: Iago occupies every social space but commits to none, switching audiences and registers without friction. Structurally he is what Coleridge called the play's 'motiveless malignity' — but the structure itself, rather than his psychology, is what keeps him invisible to the characters while the audience sees his seams.

trials.prompt: When Iago says 'I am not what I am' (line 65), what does he mean?
options: He regrets a past identity he has lost
His outward appearance is a deliberate mask concealing his true nature
He is uncertain of his own feelings about Othello
He is quoting a Biblical formula to claim divine authority

trials.prompt: Why does Iago choose to rouse Brabantio at midnight by shouting beneath his window rather than approaching him directly in daylight?
options: Venice's laws forbid private citizens from visiting senators by day
Panic in the dark makes Brabantio more likely to react before thinking, spreading scandal Iago need not own
He fears Othello will intercept him if he moves openly
He is under orders from Roderigo, who pays him by the hour

trials.prompt: The animal imagery Iago uses ('black ram', 'Barbary horse', 'beast with two backs') serves primarily to:
options: Amuse Roderigo, who is a rural gentleman fond of hunting metaphors
Describe Cassio's relative youth and inexperience as a soldier
Racialize Othello's marriage as unnatural bestial coupling rather than human union
Express Iago's genuine love of animals and the outdoors

trials.prompt: Iago calls Othello's rank 'his Moorship's ancient' (line 32). What does this coinage do?
options: Uses 'Moorship' as a formal Venetian military title
Mocks 'Lordship' by substituting 'Moor', demeaning Othello's authority through a racial pun
Reveals that Iago genuinely respects Othello's North African ancestry
Signals that Othello has been recently knighted by the Doge

trials.prompt: Iago complains that Cassio was promoted ahead of him because of 'letter and affection' rather than 'the old gradation'. What is the 'old gradation'?
options: Seniority-based promotion through ranks
Hereditary nobility passed from father to son
The exam-based promotion system of the Venetian navy
The order in which soldiers enter the battlefield

trials.prompt: What does Iago finally advise Roderigo to do with his money?
options: Loan it to Brabantio to secure Desdemona's hand
Buy Cassio's commission directly from him
Give it to Iago to keep and to use against Othello
Send it to his family in Verona for safekeeping
